1.matchText: the worst famine in human history
1.blurb: The Great Leap Forward of 1958-62 caused the deadliest famine in history, killing tens of millions; Mao's responsibility for it is the '30 percent wrong' the post-Mao party had to bury to keep its legitimacy.

1.matchText: arrested Mao's wife and her three allies
1.blurb: Weeks after Mao's death in September 1976, Hua Guofeng arrested Jiang Qing and the three Shanghai radicals, ending the Cultural Revolution by force and opening the vacuum Deng would fill.

2.matchText: the structure Mao had built in the Great Leap Forward
2.blurb: Mao reorganized the countryside into giant collective people's communes in 1958; the abolition of household farming and the link between effort and reward is exactly what the Household Responsibility System reversed twenty years later.

3.blurb: Meiji Japan built railways, banks, and modern industry by state direction from the 1870s, the original Asian model of top-down catch-up modernization that the East Asian developmental states, China included, descend from.

3.matchText: from war ruin to industrial power
3.blurb: South Korea under Park Chung-hee went from postwar devastation to an industrial economy in roughly a generation via a state-directed export model — the 'Miracle on the Han' that prefigured and partly inspired the Chinese coastal strategy.

5.blurb: Mao declared the People's Republic from Tiananmen in October 1949 with 'the Chinese people have stood up'; forty years later the same square became the site of the movement the same party crushed with the army.

7.matchText: the First Opium War and the Treaty of Nanking
7.blurb: Britain took Hong Kong from the Qing in the 1842 Treaty of Nanking that ended the First Opium War and opened the 'century of humiliation'; the 1997 handover was framed as the literal reversal of that opening defeat.

7.matchText: the high-growth economies of Southeast Asia
7.blurb: The 1997-98 Asian Financial Crisis hammered the open, fast-growing economies of Southeast Asia and South Korea (whose chaebol collapsed into a 58-billion-dollar IMF bailout); China, with a closed capital account, was insulated and drew the contrast for a generation.

8.matchText: India in these same years was also growing fast
8.blurb: India's economy took off after its 1991 liberalization, but as a democracy where the rural poor vote, it could never impose a hukou-style disenfranchisement of its migrant workers — the slower, messier, less coercive path that is this series' central comparison.

9.blurb: Japan's asset bubble burst in 1990 into decades of stagnation; China passed Japan as the world's second-largest economy in 2010 while telling itself it had studied and would avoid the Japanese trap.

9.matchText: the century of humiliation that opened the previous book
9.blurb: The 'century of humiliation' is conventionally dated from the First Opium War; the 2008 Olympics were explicitly staged as the announcement that it was over and China had resumed first-rank status.

11.matchText: the same emotional engine Mao had used in 1949
11.blurb: Mao fused national pride to party rule with 'the Chinese people have stood up' in 1949; Xi's 'great rejuvenation' attached the same century-of-humiliation emotion to a rich and rising country rather than a poor and wounded one.

11.matchText: legitimacy and succession had no stable form
11.blurb: The Qing dynasty fell in 1912 in part because the imperial system had no stable mechanism for legitimacy and succession; Deng's term limits solved that problem, and the 2018 abolition of them deliberately reopened it.

13.matchText: the losing side of the Chinese Revolution fled in 1949
13.blurb: Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists withdrew to Taiwan after losing the civil war in 1949; the island then became a democracy, and the crushing of Hong Kong's 'two systems' destroyed the model meant to win it back peacefully.

14.matchText: the 1962 war China fought while its own people starved
14.blurb: China fought and won a short Himalayan border war against India in 1962 in the middle of the Great Leap famine; the 2020 Galwan clash was a deadly echo on the same contested frontier.

India and China began their modern histories at roughly the same point of poverty around 1949-50 and took opposite political roads — electoral democracy versus one-party rule — the great natural experiment this series has tracked for seventy years.

15.matchText: the question the Qing died on
15.blurb: The Qing collapsed on the question of how an old, proud, enormous civilization absorbs the power of the modern world without losing itself; this book is one lifetime's astonishing, unfinished answer to that exact question.

15.matchText: the abdication of a child emperor
15.blurb: The Chinese Revolution opened on the February 1912 abdication of the six-year-old Puyi, ending two thousand years of imperial rule; this book closes the chain that began in that vacuum.
